"[\"Insufficient financial management capacity within Solomon Power\",\"Recruitment of design and supervision consultants (DSC) delayed\",\"Insufficient procurement capacity in the PMU to manage ADB procurement procedures\",\"Low capacity of local contractors results in poor-quality installations\",\"Proposed technology is new to Solomon Power, which may not have technical skills to operate and maintain the systems\",\"Major customers disconnect from grid and self-generate\",\"Staffing of the PMU is not sufficient to manage project implementation adequately\",\"Solomon Power is unable to retain trained staff to operate the solar-diesel hybrids\",\"Failure to secure land during initial phases of development\",\"Lack of provincial government support during implementation\"]"

"Risks to the project not achieving overall success include: changes in the regulatory environment or power purchasing agreement (PPA), particularly concerning curtailment, changes in law, and termination scenarios; parties not meeting contractual obligations; climate and weather risks; construction delays from force majeure; and cost overruns. Other risks stem from potential failures in enhancing environmental and social measures or institutional capacity for compliance with ADB safeguards. Specific environment, health, and safety performance gaps include the absence of a safety officer, unsafe practices, improper waste management, poor housekeeping, and inadequate traffic and security. Furthermore, risks involve the failure to implement a livelihood restoration plan or an effective grievance redress mechanism, and issues related to corruption, money laundering, and financing of terrorism."

"Risks to the activity's overall success include: 1. Stakeholders in the river basins do not cooperate to operationalize the flood warning and management system in Hoi An. 2. Individual developers in the Bao Ninh new urban area do not follow the measures to protect the coastal dune complex. 3. Inadequate subsidies are provided for wastewater and water supply before achieving cost recovery. 4. Staff assigned to project by government are frequently rotated."

"Lack of either access to, clear ownership of, or usage rights for the steam resources could limit further investment in geothermal energy. Transmission and distribution networks expand more slowly than needed. Broader resource risks inherent in any geothermal project materialize. Implementation delays may be caused by lack of timely availability of budgeted resources and by lengthy approval processes for land acquisition and safeguards compliance."

"[\"The long-term viability of each subproject is linked to the financial health of state-level distribution companies, most of which have moderate-to-weak credit risk profiles and rely heavily on timely tariff subsidies, leading to a high risk of delayed payments. The impact of government initiatives to improve their financial standing remains to be seen.\",\"There is no assurance that swap markets can support the envisaged foreign exchange hedges at the time funds are required, necessitating alternative Indian rupee funding mechanisms. Costs related to unwinding swaps will be passed on to the SPV if early repayment occurs.\",\"Capacity utilization rates do not meet forecast because solar or wind resources are less than anticipated.\",\"Power production falls short of plans because of accidents and delayed or inadequate maintenance.\",\"Generated energy is not dispatched because transmission capacity at substation delivery point is inadequate.\",\"Land acquisition is delayed.\",\"Changes to prices of supplies and components.\",\"Trained human resources are not available.\"]"

"[\"Legal and regulatory risk in the geothermal subsector.\",\"PLN has not started the permitting or AMDAL process for the associated transmission line and substation, and no impact consideration was undertaken.\",\"Threats to biodiversity primarily result from community exploitation of forest fauna and flora.\",\"Power production falling short of plans due to accidents and delays in maintenance.\",\"Weak power demand because of adverse macroeconomic developments.\",\"Cost of raw material increases more than budgeted.\",\"Delayed land acquisition, particularly for PLN's associated transmission line.\",\"Unavailability of trained staff.\",\"Residual risk from reservoir performance and resource management.\",\"High drilling risk of delays or cost overruns due to unproductive wells, lower-than-expected well capacities, and unknown underground soil conditions.\",\"Construction and technology risk due to dual flash technology not being common in Indonesia.\",\"Offtaker counterparty risk with PLN.\",\"Operation and maintenance risk from geothermal fluid's composition deviating from current projections.\",\"Risk of revenue disruption from seismic activities or other natural hazards like flooding, landslides, and volcanic activity.\",\"Transmission risk from PLN's delays in completing the requisite transmission line and switchyard or underperformance in acceleration programs.\"]"

"Risks include: Delay/partial completion & lack of capacity/resources; weak local utilities & poor environmental monitoring/regulation; failure to address non-point pollution/low river flows; inadequate institutional framework & systemic weaknesses in urban wastewater/solid waste management; insufficient investment scale/sustainability; poor social engagement; inadequate industrial wastewater capacity/regulation; challenging program scale; inappropriate subproject siting; inadequate waste/sludge disposal; poor construction environmental management; inadequate facility maintenance; overlooked indirect impacts."

"Current institutional arrangements at the national level are not sufficiently coordinated, leading to overlapping functions, supply-driven service delivery, and insufficient attention to O&M and user involvement. Union Parishads lack technical capacity to lead the sector. NGO involvement causes fragmented service approaches, weak delivery mechanisms, inadequate hygiene promotion, and lack of user ownership, threatening sustainability. Unclear roles between central DPHE and local government institutions exist. Rural piped water systems require higher investment costs and new, unfamiliar institutional management and O&M models. Failure to establish a computerized accounting system and a Project Management Unit by specified legal covenant dates."

"Weak national ownership and insufficient collaboration with the sectoral ministries concerned by the programme, deterioration of the national socio-political situation, and the take-off of the 5th wave of COVID-19."

"[\"Weak institutional capacity to support bottom-up participatory approaches, multi-sector local investment planning, and subnational government capacity building, within the context of ongoing reforms and government restructuring.\", \"Weak capacity of regional hokimiyats (through UCS engineering companies) in technical design and procurement practices.\", \"Social risks, including media reports that forced labor and involuntary resettlement are occurring under the Obod Qishloq state program.\", \"The institutionalization of community and civil society participation in local "Relevant risks include: little private sector interest in PPP for irrigation; uncertain financing for recurrent operation and maintenance of project assets after project completion; prolonged COVID-19 impact causing delays in civil works and M-IMO recruitment; cultural barriers limiting women's participation due to overt discouragement and strict observance of purdah; weak financial management capacity possibly leading to material misstatements and qualified audit reports; and future climate change impact exceeding projections in terms of temperature, precipitation, and sea level rise."

"[\"Low plant availability, reduced efficiency, and deficient operation and maintenance technology cause disruptions.\",\"Appropriately skilled women are not available to take up operational jobs.\",\"Severe and abrupt capital market or economy disruptions\",\"Noncompliance with engineering, procurement, and construction contractual obligations\",\"Delay in construction caused by force majeure events\",\"Price of raw material increases more than budgeted.\"]"

"The program faces significant development financing needs, with a remaining gap of approximately $3.2 billion and an additional $2.2 billion required for 2017. Other risks include weak subprovincial performance of public financial management and slow fund disbursement, identified gaps in monitoring system capacity, inadequate regulatory enforcement at subprovincial levels, a weak environmental institutional framework, and incomplete regulations. Furthermore, there's a risk that air pollution control may no longer be a priority for HPG, or that municipal governments and the private sector may not support HPG's efforts. The prevalence of \"Two High\" industries (high-energy-consumption and high-pollutant-emission), low utilization of clean energy due to short supply of natural gas, and past insufficient pollution control measures pose further challenges. Industrial transformation's success also hinges on adequate social protection and employment opportunities for affected laborers."

"Risks: Power purchase agreement unenforceability (worsening security in Afghanistan); equipment transportation/damage; manufactured equipment fitting issues (delays, claims); project implementation delays/cost overruns; flood; staffing deficiencies/insufficient capacity for procurement/contract administration; weak financial management/audits; operation and maintenance challenges; untimely receipt of qualified bids for turnkey contract; ineffective Barki Tojik corporate restructuring/utility operations efficiency not achieved."

"1. LWUA and participating water districts will not have sufficient capacity to utilize funds during implementation; reluctance of water districts to borrow, especially for sanitation. 2. Operation of new infrastructure is not sustainable because of low tariffs, poor collections, inefficient operations, and/or limited human resources. 3. Water districts have limited capacity in financial management and procurement of ODA-funded projects, and managing foreign exchange risk on loans. 4. Potential integrity risks at LWUA. 5. High turnover rate of trained staff. 6. Construction delays and a lack of quantifiable targets (identified as issues in a previous partially successful project). 7. Limited private investment in the Philippine water sector due to scale and regulatory risk."

"[\"Potential for a one-year project delay, 5% increase in project costs, and 5% decrease in financial benefits, as highlighted in a sensitivity analysis, could impact the financial outcomes.\",\"Risk of lack of timely completion of two linked Water Treatment Plants (WTPs) financed by local funds in Fuxin.\",\"Challenges related to the proper implementation and sustainability of the large number of innovations introduced, including MIS, SCADA, telemetry systems, and online monitoring platforms.\",\"Potential delay in procurement of goods, technologies, or software required for the project.\",\"Reliance on ongoing financial support from the Government is critical for utilities to achieve full cost recovery, as current tariffs are insufficient, posing a risk if such support is not sustained.\",\"Lack of incentives for improving service quality at the utilities could hinder the sustained operational efficiency improvements.\",\"Risk that water utilities continue to employ a reactive leakage management strategy, repairing failures reported less timely, which could prevent achieving Non-Revenue Water (NRW) reduction goals.\"]"

"[\"A potential risk is a delay in implementation, which would postpone the project benefits.\", \"Macroeconomic targets may not be achieved.\", \"Associated facilities and infrastructure may not be developed.\", \"The traffic forecast for Zamyn Uud may not be realized.\", \"Shippers and freight operators may not realize the benefits of using the facility.\", \"The government may not provide funds in a timely manner.\"]"

"Economic activities are adversely affected by natural disasters and lack of climate change impacts. Municipalities are not prepared to collect revenues for operation and maintenance of urban infrastructure. Project start-up delays increase the number of affected people along the embankment alignment. Civil society or nongovernment organizations raise concerns during project implementation. Dhamnak Chheukrom Irrigation System Rehabilitation works (upstream) does not lower flood levels of Pursat river in town center after completion."

"[\"Financial viability could be at risk if a 10% increase in capital costs and a 10% decrease in benefits occur simultaneously with a 1-year delay in increasing wastewater tariffs.\",\"Prolonged government approval procedures by oversight agencies delay project implementation.\",\"The non-implementation of tariff increases by the local government due to political and/or economic reasons.\",\"Tariff increases are lower than expected.\",\"Low-quality bidder participation, particularly for design-build contracts.\",\"Insufficient institutional capacity of implementing agency (TST) may cause progress delay.\",\"Staff turnover (in TST) may cause progress delay.\"]"

"Key risks include the extremely vast and unsafe supervision area; differences in country legislations on wildlife management and lack of communication/information sharing; inadequate institutional capacity; uncontrolled and sophisticated poaching by heavily armed trans-border criminal organizations, driven by international ivory demand and hindered by lack of resources/coordination; disruption from pastoral and mining activities; insufficient political commitment from States for trans-border security; and the ineffectiveness of localized interventions against mobile poachers, potentially leading to ecological fragmentation."

"[\"Historical operating inefficiency of electricity distribution companies due to high non-technical losses from informal connections and high operating costs.\",\"Challenges in the institutional framework for ensuring rational and efficient use of resources, requiring institutionalized planning for efficient power generation and transmission expansion, competitive processes for new generation, viable renewable energy options, rural electrification development, and a National Energy Efficiency Program.\",\"The need for legal framework changes to achieve universal, quality, efficient, reliable, resilient, environmentally friendly, and financially sustainable electricity service, as emphasized by the Electricity Pact.\",\"Reliance on a stable macroeconomic framework, as the fiscal accounts were significantly weakened by the pandemic, leading to structural deficits in the nonfinancial public sector (NFPS) and increased national debt, with the power sector being a main contributor to these deficits and debt.\",\"Gaps in women's participation in the energy sector, characterized by lower pay, fewer benefits, and lack of contracts in some cases, and the absence of a strategy to increase women's participation and improve gender equity.\",\"Gaps in the participation and inclusion of persons with disabilities in the energy sector, marked by economic inactivity, lack of workplace integration policies, and absence of incentives for service providers to offer inclusive services to customers.\",\"Vulnerability of electricity loss reduction efforts to external shocks, as demonstrated by the deterioration of indicators during the COVID-19 pandemic, which pushed losses up to 40% before recovery.\",\"Historical rising trend in operating expenses for electricity distribution companies from 2013-2019, indicating a persistent challenge in cost management despite a recent fall.\"]"

"Potential risks to the activity not achieving overall success include a \"rebound effect\" where energy savings are offset by increased consumption. Additionally, EESL's ambitious growth into new business segments and geographic expansion could strain its financial and human resources, potentially delaying the release and deployment of its counterpart contribution. Counterparty risks are significant, as most of EESL's project payments come from urban local bodies and distribution companies with uncertain cash flows, leading to delayed payments and a substantial increase in EESL's trade receivables from FY2017 to FY2019. Furthermore, financial reporting issues were flagged by external auditors, including a significant increase in receivables balances, inadequate provisioning for doubtful trade receivables, inconsistent accounting policies, and potential accounting adjustments at the conclusion of ongoing reconciliations of receivable and/or payable balances, assets, and inventories, which could result in a restatement of financial accounts."

"[\"Possibility of major outbreaks of violence caused by other armed group and other violent extremist groups in the Bangsamoro region.\", \"Differences in political and religious perspectives among project stakeholders leading to difficulties in project implementation.\", \"Local environment, such as leader (religious, political, civic) attitude and practices, may not be supportive to capacity-development initiatives on dialogue, mediation, conflict resolution and other initiatives on prevention of violent extremism (e.g., common framework for Islamic teaching and preaching).\", \"Mobility restrictions due to COVID-19.\", \"Skeletal workforce in BARMM focused on relief and emergency operations, which may affect government commitment or capacity to co-implement identified activities.\", \"Availability of stakeholders to be part of the KIIs and FGDs for the socioeconomic impact assessment.\", \"Limited internet connectivity in BARMM which may affect response rate to SEIA surveys.\"]"

"Risks include low industrial sector commitment to wastewater reuse due to low cost, marketplace owners' unwillingness to integrate CE sections, low manufacturer commitment to circular design due to potential cost increase, and challenges with recycled plastic (informal recyclers' safety reluctance, formal industry's quality concerns). Further risks are a persistent lack of CE guidelines, limited technology access, low SME capacity, an insufficient enabling environment, and the absence of specific legal/financial frameworks for eco-design and sectoral CE standards."

"[\"Extreme climate events and flash floods may disrupt or delay the execution of works.\",\"Price escalations because of the global economic situation could delay procurement of goods and services, and slow project implementation.\",\"There is high potential for grievances from workers and local communities due to the urban location.\",\"The project's financial sustainability is at moderate risk, depending on tariffs remaining cost-reflective.\",\"Moderate pre-mitigation financial management risk stemming from DESCO's qualified audit opinion on its latest financial statements (discrepancy in workers' profit participation fund liability) and historical issues in audited project financial statements for previous ADB-funded projects, including a qualified audit opinion, unsubmitted expenditure statements, and unresolved audit observations.\"]"

"Risks include: a substantial lack of a regulatory/policy ecosystem and uncertain code/standard adjustments affecting operations/revenue; SECI's limited track record in large-scale plant development/operation, implying capacity constraints; substantial fiduciary risks from financial systems, governance, and procurement experience. Economic viability is highly sensitive to commissioning delays, CUF uncertainty, curtailment, and reliance on environmental externalities. Land scarcity, off-taker financial conditions, and non-payment by utilities also pose risks."

"Risks to overall success include: negative environmental impacts during construction (e.g., increased dust, air pollution, noise, vibration, water pollution, local traffic volume, safety risks for local communities and businesses, UXO risks); risks during operation if infrastructures are not properly designed, maintained adequately, or sluices are not properly operated; and potential negative social impacts due to land acquisition, which could affect up to 3,000 households."

"[\"Construction cost overruns or delays due to geological surprises, unexpected price escalation, natural disasters, or unforeseen geological conditions during implementation.\",\"Offtaker's credit risk impacting the power purchase agreement.\",\"Unavailability of the nearest substation or failure to connect the transmission line, preventing power evacuation.\",\"Adverse impacts of unmitigated climate change or natural disasters, despite adaptation measures in design.\",\"Failure to adhere to safeguard policies or issues arising from social/environmental impacts.\",\"Currency mismatch risk impacting foreign debt service under severe stress tests.\",\"Global or regional economic slowdown reducing demand for energy.\",\"Prices of construction materials increasing beyond provisioned amounts.\",\"Failure to establish a satisfactory subsidiary financing arrangement, preventing agreements from becoming effective.\",\"Failure to establish a satisfactory power purchase agreement, hindering ADB fund disbursement.\",\"Indian commercial bank loans not becoming effective, impacting ADB fund disbursement.\",\"Failure to establish a satisfactory cofinancing arrangement with Indian commercial banks, hindering ADB fund disbursement.\"]"

"[\"Escalating prices for construction materials and project cost overrun potential due to high inflation.\",\"Moderate to substantial financial sustainability risk for the non-revenue earning project due to its fiscal impact on MRTD's budget, given the country's economic difficulties and spending restrictions, and insufficient allocated budget for routine maintenance.\",\"MRTD's limited financial management capacity for externally funded projects.\",\"Limited procurement and contract management capacity of SPA and MRTD, including lack of contract management capacity and unpredictable bidder participation.\",\"Vulnerability of the road network to extreme weather events such as flooding and landslides, which regularly render roads and bridges impassable for long periods, and increasing permafrost melt causing subsidence and damage to road surfaces.\",\"Potential temporary and site-specific environmental impacts during construction (noise, dust, emissions, waste, biodiversity loss) and operation (noise, community health and safety risks, increased traffic, potential wildlife collisions).\"]"

"[\"Weak administrative and institutional capacity of sector ministries and government systems, lacking sufficient staff and equipment, and facing political resistance, can hinder reform implementation, environmental due diligence enforcement, and overall results.\",\"Macroeconomic risks include vulnerability to commodity price and weather shocks, which can depress revenues and affect economic priorities, alongside global financial volatility and the gradual tightening of G3 central bank monetary policy.\",\"Intensified terrorist activities and persisting security concerns in Niger and neighboring countries threaten to impact production, investment, exports, divert public spending, disrupt economic activities, and negatively affect program implementation and outcomes.\",\"Niger remains at moderate risk of external debt distress, with the debt-to-exports ratio breaching thresholds under export shock stress tests, and the public debt-to-GDP ratio potentially trending upwards if the primary deficit remains constant.\",\"Challenges in fiscal management and tax reform implementation, including difficulties in increasing tax revenue, sustaining fiscal consolidation, implementing future tax reforms (as evidenced by protests), and weak PFM systems in budget planning, execution, and forecasting.\",\"Implementation challenges for specific DPF actions, such as the payment of arrears to extractive industry-affected communes, given the budget's vulnerability to exogenous shocks.\",\"Inefficiencies in critical infrastructure, specifically in the power and ICT sectors.\",\"Low agricultural productivity due to limited adoption of modern inputs like improved seeds and fertilizer, coupled with environmental risks from unregulated agricultural inputs.\",\"Potential adverse social and environmental impacts on local communities from the influx of workers for civil works (e.g., rural road maintenance), and environmental destruction linked to unregulated artisanal mining activities.\"]"

"Risks to the activity not achieving overall success include: lack of support from some government groups and opposition from some NGOs/academic groups to sector reforms; fraud and corruption in procurement/financial transactions; inadequate capacity in procurement, financial, and safeguards management; risks with new fund flow mechanisms; challenging inter-governmental and cross-sectoral coordination among over 100 implementing agencies; potential impacts on indigenous groups and small-scale land acquisition; temporary localized adverse environmental impacts (e.g., erosion, water quality deterioration) from physical works; improper environmental management, relocation, or compensation; increased agricultural input use (pesticides); risks related to Jatiluhur dam safety; and lack of coordination among different donors."

"Political and Governance risks, Macroeconomic risks, and Fiduciary risks are rated as substantial. Sector Strategies and Policies risks, Technical Design of Project or Program risks, Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability risks, Environment and Social risks, and Stakeholders risks are rated as moderate. Specific environmental risks from small-scale construction works include dust and noise generation, waste management issues, material supply challenges, and worker safety concerns. There is a potential risk of involuntary land acquisition or resettlement, which would necessitate the preparation and approval of a Resettlement Action Plan. Additionally, works involving maintenance of specific bridges of historical value or age could impact physical cultural resources, requiring direct involvement of monument and heritage protection authorities."

"Risks to success include: (1) Possible non-compliance and delays in delivering verification means and in the agreed form (monitoring and accountability risk). (2) Possible delays in contracting and executing consultancies for committed activities (development risk). (3) Potential adaptations of future activating mechanisms due to new knowledge, given the complexity and deadlines of implementing reforms."

"Financial risks include delayed/eliminated ICE subsidies, reallocation of MEF/MIDUVI subsidy funds affecting long-term financial position, and a 15% cut in operating revenues from 50% subsidy elimination (2008) necessitating postponed storm sewer investment and new charges. A financial mismatch exists between company obligations and operating revenues. Institutional risks: political interference causing service deterioration, lack of political will for O&M union protests, and gaps in environmental legislation delaying authorizations. The responsible agency might not enforce the slope management plan, and rates may not cover costs."

"The overall risk of the operation is high, with the possibility that political commitment may weaken, social tensions could increase, or natural disasters may derail fiscal consolidation efforts and set back the reform program. The Jamaican economy is highly vulnerable to external shocks and natural disasters, exacerbated by a large current account deficit, low foreign exchange reserves, high unemployment, and limited fiscal space, which reduces the Government's flexibility to absorb unforeseen shocks. There is a governance and political risk that the heavy reform agenda could lose momentum if negative impacts reduce political and popular support or if vested interests intervene, given that reforms under the IMF EFF have already significantly depleted the government's political capital and provoked public debate. Lastly, capacity risks are present as the broad reform agenda stretches government capacity and fiscal resources thin, potentially causing delayed implementation and weak enforcement of new legislation. Legislative capacity is strained by the volume of new laws, and a public sector wage freeze hampers the ability to attract and retain talent for implementing reforms."

"[\"Artisanal fishers may not adopt new fishing techniques or be less inclined to venture into the open sea.\",\"Poor weather conditions affecting fishing trips.\",\"A potential fall in fish prices if increased catch leads to oversupply.\",\"Fish Aggregating Devices (FADs) being carried away during rough seas or cyclones.\",\"Difficulty in developing a sufficiently simple and user-friendly application for fishers to use advanced fishing technology.\",\"Some fishers may have problems interpreting images from a graphic interface used in the new technology.\",\"Damage to newly constructed infrastructure through vandalism.\",\"Annual rainfall levels may be too low for rainwater harvesting systems to be effective throughout the year.\",\"Existing fish landing station roofs may be unable to hold solar PV panels.\",\"Output of solar-powered/ice-making machines may be dependent on insolation levels, affecting their effectiveness.\",\"Non-availability of sufficient space in the port region for new cold room or industrial ice machine facilities.\",\"A small number of targeted trainees may not be interested in following the training or awareness campaigns.\",\"A higher catch-per-unit-effort could increase fishing efficiency and potentially lead to overexploitation of fish stocks in an unmanaged fishery.\",\"Non-adaptation to new technology may lead to social exclusion and create a category of 'un-adapted' fishers using older systems.\"]"

"Risks: WSM projects may achieve planned activities but fail to deliver desired downstream results; Reservoir sedimentation can reduce expected benefits and affect optimal dam operation; High exposure to droughts, floods, and extreme precipitation; High inflows and large sediment volumes in reservoirs due to intense rainfall and erosion; Interruptions to water flows disrupting irrigation and drinking water supplies; Encroachment into irrigation canal and access road right-of-way; Lack of institutional collaboration and sustained WSM mechanisms post-project; High macroeconomic vulnerabilities (weak fiscal buffers, indebtedness, refinancing needs, reduced public investment); Prolonged COVID-19 outbreak hampering growth and fiscal sustainability; Complexity of technical design, especially for WSM and iterative watershed approach; Limited institutional capacity for WSM planning under Component 1; Significant and complex remedial works for large dams."

"[\"The complexity of urban challenges in HCMC, including quality of growth, human resources, public administration, urban settlement, traffic congestion, urban flooding, and environmental pollution, could impede the activity's overall success.\",\"Lack of concerted actions in urban planning, municipal finance, quality of basic and advanced services, infrastructure development, and poverty and social development could hinder further success.\",\"Slowing budget revenue growth and limited revenue policy and administration autonomy pose financial risks if not adequately addressed through unlocking public asset value and other revenue sources.\",\"Potential issues with government ownership of proposed measures or unforeseen feasibility challenges could impede the activity's success.\",\"Underlying political and technical feasibility challenges, as well as limitations in implementation capacity, could materialize as risks despite being considered in the program design.\",\"Uncertainty regarding the distributional effects of policy actions, particularly concerning zoning and public transportation, poses a risk of negative welfare impacts requiring further analysis and mitigation.\",\"Increased operating costs for businesses from higher rentals of city-owned commercial housing could negatively affect enterprise viability or welfare if additional revenues are not distributed favorably.\",\"Asymmetric and negative second-order distributional effects from online disclosure of zoning plans, specifically identifying zoning violations and imposing penalties impacting property valuation, represent a risk with undetermined welfare effects.\",\"Some individuals, particularly in low-density areas, may be negatively affected by public transportation route re-organization, despite an overall expected positive welfare effect with mitigation measures.\"]"

"[\"Insufficient electricity tariff levels and the suspension of price increases since 2004 have deteriorated PLN's financial viability, leading to unsustainable government subsidies and weakening its ability to cover supply costs.\",\"Restructuring and institutional reform of PLN remains in flux with inconsistent, slow progress due to legal uncertainty, weakening the government's ability to provide public financing for power sector development and deterring private investment.\",\"Rapid increase of coal in the generation fuel mix, projected to reach 70% by 2020, exposes Indonesia to significant local and global environmental risks, including heightened greenhouse gas emissions and impacts in populated/sensitive areas.\",\"Lack of incentives, regulatory certainty, and weak institutional capacity hinder the rapid development of Indonesia's abundant indigenous and clean energy resources (geothermal, hydropower, biomass), impacting the broader energy supply context.\",\"Tremendous pressure on the power sector to keep pace with rapid and sustained electricity demand growth (projected 7-9% annually), driven by economic growth, could overwhelm the project's capacity expansion efforts if not adequately managed.\"]"

"Risks to success include Uganda's systemic failure to provide reliable, cost-effective electricity; weak sector finances from inadequate tariffs and high system losses (>30%); weak institutional setup, capacity, and coordination among key players (MEMD, UETCL, REA, LDCs); LDCs' lack of incentive to expand to rural areas; high connection costs requiring upfront payment from consumers; inadequate funding for service connection materials; delays in infrastructure development and establishing new institutional arrangements; potential future power shortages from demand growth; a significant US$1.0 billion financing gap for the sector; and lack of operators for some Service Territories."

"[\"Declining tax collection relative to GDP and dependence on hydropower revenues risk fiscal sustainability if expansionary fiscal policies continue without sufficient domestic revenue mobilization.\",\"Employment opportunities concentrated in the public sector, unequal playing field for the private sector due to pension disparities, leading to public job preference and erosion of the private sector's talent pool. "The overall risk rating for this operation is substantial. Major risks that could impact its success include: (a) political economy and governance challenges, (b) sector strategies challenges, particularly for gas, (c) environmental and social risks (especially as most geothermal potential is on forest land), (d) weak institutional and implementation capacity, and (e) uncertainty in the macroeconomic environment (global and domestic). If these materialize, they could hinder the Government's willingness/ability to implement reforms or mobilize investment. Specific environmental and social risks related to geothermal include loss of forests/ecosystems, fragmentation, pollution, disruption of natural water balance, land acquisition due to weak land tenure, and institutional/capacity gaps in enforcing legal frameworks for environmental management."

"[\"The main risks include: (i) institutional capacity due to thin public sector capacity to implement and sustain reforms; (ii) sector strategies/policies in water and education reforms; (iii) political and governance risks from politically-sensitive reform aspects; (iv) macroeconomic risks from volatile economic/fiscal situation, where large shocks could detract from policy focus; (v) environmental risks from natural disasters and climate change impacts; and (vi) fiduciary risk.\"]"

"[\"The activity faces significant risks from management weaknesses within the Ministry of Home Affairs Project Management Unit (MOHA PMU), including unfilled central-level positions and unclear functional counterparts. Unclear reporting lines, competing authority structures, and lack of coordination among PMD, NMC, and RMCs cause confusion and delays. Capacity constraints due to understaffing, insufficient training, and poor coordination undermine program systems and fiduciary controls. Additionally, poor recruitment and retention of qualified facilitators, especially in remote areas, due to inadequate remuneration scales, threaten implementation quality. The Complaints Handling System (CHS) may also be insufficiently robust for the program's increased scale, risking inadequate grievance resolution.\",\"Operational risks include patchy quality of staff training and uneven oversight of Safeguards-related issues, coupled with inadequate facilitator environmental training and potential weaknesses in sub-project engineering design. Lastly, there's a risk of engineers not adequately following land acquisition rules or villagers feeling pressure to contribute land, necessitating guideline updates and potentially leading to project cancellations or redesigns.\"]"

"[\"Escalation of attacks and further deterioration of the security situation in Ukraine, potentially leading to enhanced security restrictions that restrict staff movement, direct consultations, and project activities; potential risk of injury or detention to project staff, contractors, partners, grantees or beneficiaries impacting their ability and commitment to execute activities; and potential risk of damage to assets during transportation, storage, and installation.\",\"Continuous targeted attacks on energy infrastructure, which have already halved power generation capacity, slashed transmission capacity, damaged or destroyed numerous high-voltage transformers, and continue to cause further losses in generation capacity and substations. The current pricing decree does not provide a clear directive to pay a premium to compensate developers, establishing a ceiling price that complicates securing appropriate power purchase agreements.\", \"PLN, which is already under pressure to reduce its costs, is reluctant to off-take more expensive electricity without clear direction from the Government of Indonesia (GoI) as to the mechanism through which they will be compensated for the associated incremental cost, leading to long, drawn-out negotiations for power purchase prices that undermine efforts to scale-up geothermal development.\"]"

"[\"Public Financial Management (PFM) risks, with a moderate fiduciary risk at the PFM level, where shortcomings in public sector performance are among the main constraints to economic development.\",\"Weakening of Government of Senegal (GOS) commitment to reforming the electricity sector.\",\"Limited private sector interest in Senegal's electricity sector.\",\"Increase in SENELEC's operating costs, potentially due to high fuel costs, poor management, and poor investment decisions.\",\"Weakening of SENELEC's financial condition, attributable to unwise investment and financing decisions, higher fuel costs not compensated by tariff adjustments, a decrease in its collection rate, or the inability of oversight institutions to carry out their responsibilities.\",\"Delays in commissioning the new Independent Power Producer (IPP) and rehabilitating the electricity facilities.\"]"

"Prolonged political instability and conflict that could hinder sustained governance efforts; deep-rooted issues in governance and transparency including disrupted budget cycles, poor public financial management, corruption, lack of transparency in cocoa/petroleum sectors, and weakened accountability, posing significant reform challenges; and potential for inadequate management of future environmental and social impacts from petroleum development on marine/onshore areas, fisheries, and communities, if mitigation from the planned Social and Environmental Assessment (SEA) is not effectively implemented, undermining sustainable resource management."

"Risks include: failure to implement Environmental Management Plan mitigating actions for adverse environmental impacts; insufficient or ineffective compensatory measures for adverse effects on natural habitats and the Bonfim/Guaraira Environmental Protection Area; ineffective preparation or implementation of Integrated Pest Management Plans leading to unmanaged pest issues; challenges arising from involuntary resettlement if it occurs and the Resettlement Framework is not adequately applied; disruptions from accidental cultural heritage discovery or inadequate assessments in archaeological sites; and dam safety failures if rehabilitation recommendations are not fully implemented for the 40 strategic dams."

"Risks include: (1) Fiscal instability and resource allocation issues that may lead to permanent modifications, delays, or project stoppage, exacerbated by the current fiscal situation in Argentina. (2) Slow progress due to bureaucratic, non-transparent, and cumbersome procurement procedures. (3) Significant environmental impacts and temporary disruptions during construction, such as soil excavation, traffic disturbance, noise, dust, and soil evacuation, particularly for the two tunnels and secondary drainage works, requiring strong supervision. (4) Insufficient capacity within the implementation unit for supervising complex tunnel construction, requiring specialized expertise."

"Relevant risks include challenges in obtaining long-term financing in Cambodia due to political risk and the nascent local banking sector. The project also faces risks related to achieving gender equality and women's empowerment goals due to existing gender disparities, low literacy, and attitudes towards gender roles in the workforce. Additionally, potential environmental impacts (e.g., noise, dust, waste, soil erosion) and occupational health and safety risks during construction, if not properly managed, could hinder success."

"The pre-mitigation financial management risk is moderate. The project procurement risk is moderate. Risks to the activity not achieving overall success also include declining demand for Cambodian products due to competition from external markets and stringent quality and safety requirements from importing countries; capacity constraints for infrastructure design and procurement; climate change impacts that may adversely affect project investments; and limited attention to the operation and maintenance (O&M) of agribusiness infrastructure."

"[\"Inadequate institutional arrangements/staff, insufficient technical capacity for market-orientation/commercialization, and challenges in identifying capacity building needs are major risks. The implementing agency (CAR) lacks strong private sector links and market analysis capacity. There's a risk from the cultural/institutional shift from needs-based to market-opportunity-oriented project approaches, requiring significant capacity building for Producer Organizations and Community Associations in business development, commercial practices, and financial management.\",\"Financial management (FM) control risk is moderate due to challenges in adequately accounting for funds transferred to Producer Organizations (POs) and Community Associations (CAs). There is a risk of insufficient appropriately skilled resources and processes to adequately design and implement the Project at the subproject level, stemming from a lack of appropriate FM institutional arrangements within POs and CAs, and a need to enhance controls and reporting within subprojects.\"]"

Risks: Vast scale & increasing climate change impacts on fires, slow ecosystem recovery. Fragmented DRR institutions, poor coordination. Underfunding of local DRR, focus on relief over prevention, insufficient financial support for local units, prevention, detection. Limited local capacity: minimal equipment, no PPE, inadequate vehicles, poor training/incentives. Deficient fire prediction/early detection: insufficient/non-automated weather stations, reliance on satellites. Poor data management, varied reporting, no vegetation data for modeling. Procurement risks: insufficient service providers, exchange rate fluctuations, UNDP delays. 95% human-induced fires require behavioral change.

The project faces risks including: triggered safeguard policies (Environmental Assessment, Natural Habitats, Physical Cultural Resources, Involuntary Resettlement, Forests, Safety of Dams, Projects on International Waterways); potential for inadequate financial contributions and resources for operation/maintenance, including payment of arrears from countries; severe environmental degradation from poor land/water management and over-exploitation; inadequate operation and maintenance of existing water infrastructure due to funding, planning, and maintenance issues; and competitive unilateral development among Basin countries, coupled with the Niger Basin Authority's (NBA) historical inefficiencies and lack of robust legal instruments for effective cooperation.

Substantial risks include technical design and environmental/social aspects. Moderate risks involve political/governance, macroeconomic, sector strategies, institutional capacity, fiduciary, and stakeholders. Specific challenges include the Cutzamala System's lack of timely water information, absence of an automated management system, limited operational flexibility, and the need for significant rehabilitation of older dams due to safety deficiencies. Inefficient energy use and continued groundwater overexploitation causing land subsidence are also risks. Project success is contingent on government support, CONAGUA's budget alignment, and personnel technical capacity.

Risks include potential financing shortfalls for planned infrastructure, as seen in past operations due to withdrawn financing and economic crisis; delays in execution times for solid waste management by OPDS; COMIREC's current lack of critical management tools (water resource information system, environmental monitoring plan) and potential coordination issues with Autoridad del Agua (ADA); and a medium risk in the execution capacity for the administration of goods and services. The ambitious scope of restoring an entire river basin and the need for effective stakeholder management also pose challenges.

Risks: Weak institutional capacity of MNRE/SWMA; Insufficient revenue for full cost recovery, with user fees not covering recurrent costs and collection dependent on linking fees to water bills; Land acquisition delays/failure, requiring legal possession evidence before bidding; Diversion of Environmental Tax revenues from solid waste projects to central government budget; Failure to timely develop, adopt, or implement the cost recovery mechanism; Delays in Bank approval for DBO contract or Environmental Compliance Plan (ECP); Historical project cancellations due to funding issues.

Belize's high climate vulnerability (sea level rise, extreme weather, rising temperatures) and destruction of natural coastal capital (mangroves, coral reefs) lead to increased storm surges and flooding. Previous erosion control in Caye Caulker failed due to lack of structural barriers, and unsustainable practices worsen erosion there and in Goff's Caye. The tourism sector faces significantly escalating disaster losses without intervention. Belize's limited fiscal resources are strained by post-disaster rehabilitation. Belize City suffers recurrent severe flooding, hindering development.

Persistent and intense bark beetle epidemic; increased control costs due to delayed early action; bureaucratic slowness in approving timber salvage procedures leading to unusable wood; permanent/temporary loss of ecosystem services from inadequate SFM, land-use change, or erosion; national deficit in water regulation infrastructure; P. oocarpa's ecological vulnerabilities (slow growth, disease, fire, drought); low baseline of formally managed forest areas (4.5%); and a slow start to restoration activities.

IDAAN's weak management (financial unsustainability, unupdated water rates, poor governance, unqualified staff, inadequate monitoring/metering, high non-revenue water 48%); widespread service quality issues (discontinuity, pressure, quality, low metering); deteriorating infrastructure; operational deficiencies; low sewerage coverage (26% nationally, 15% western provinces); potential localized environmental/social impacts due to weak IDAAN capacity; and medium procurement risk (inconsistent evaluations, red tape, poor contract administration). Significant implementation delays indicated by zero fund disbursement and no progress on key indicators by mid-2016.

The new administration in Peru, which took office in July 2011, implies a medium risk relating to the maintenance of strategic lines of action in climate change, which could necessitate revisions to the policy commitments of the third programmatic operation. Additionally, there is a medium risk concerning public management and governance, given the high complexity and cross-cutting nature of climate change and the possible splintering of responsibilities among different sectors.

["Significant unfunded budget. Ongoing violent conflict, substantial territorial gains by IS, and IS control over critical resources like water/oil/electricity. Weakened central government, ISF overwhelmed by insurgents, violence and human rights violations by ISF/militias, and perceived discrimination bolstering anti-government sentiments. Less permissible delivery environment due to security constraints, leading to a lack of international presence in many high-risk locations/sectors. Significant gaps in humanitarian coordination and assistance.", "Increased tensions between displaced and host communities, exacerbated by labor market competition and ineffective conflict resolution. Exhausted host community coping mechanisms and local government struggles to provide equitable services under overwhelming demand. Gaps in service provision due to lack of funds, low IDP access to education/health, and unawareness of services. High poverty, female/youth unemployment, and limited women's empowerment due to violence, discriminatory laws, and harmful practices. Environmental risks include increased solid/sewage waste, water/electricity shortages, and overstretched health centers."]

["Unplanned urban growth, weak institutional capacity, and lack of integrated planning and inter-sectoral coordination pose significant risks. Deficient planning and financial frameworks hinder sustainable development. The chaotic transport system, poor public transport quality, lack of multimodal options, and low compliance with traffic regulations lead to congestion, accidents, and higher emissions. Gender inequality, low female participation in municipal governance, and weak gender mainstreaming frameworks are also significant challenges to success.", "Risks include insufficient waste management regulation compliance, limited technical capacity for specialized waste streams, and low public awareness. Only 68% waste collection leads to illegal dumping, environmental contamination (GHG, UPOPs, soil/water), and floods. Unmonitored hazardous waste and open burning produce toxic pollutants. Green infrastructure faces critical gaps, lack of coordination, absence of management plans, and insufficient financial mechanisms. Biodiversity is threatened by habitat degradation, fragmentation, and damage from transport expansion."]

Risks include: inadequate medium-to-long-term adaptation information and geo-spatial data; limited national institutional and technical capacity for CCA planning and monitoring; weak inter-ministerial and local coordination, leading to institutional memory loss; challenges in tracking adaptation investments and viable funding; difficulty achieving inter-sectoral synergy and multi-level government consensus on implementation modality; and project delays evidenced by past extensions and outdated financial planning requiring updates.

The activity faces significant risks due to insufficient technical and financial capacity for expanding and effectively managing protected areas. This is compounded by a poorly functioning institutional framework for forest management, characterized by outdated concepts and inefficient regional government units, hindering necessary capacity strengthening. Furthermore, insufficient data and poor coordination among national stakeholders for biodiversity monitoring and wildlife law enforcement, including for transboundary species, impede comprehensive analysis and action. Frequent government policy changes and personnel shifts also risk delaying national results.

Increasing extreme weather (cyclones, floods, droughts) frequency/intensity and projected temperature rise (1-4.5°C) and precipitation fall (10-35%) threaten the activity. Ahuachapán is highly vulnerable due to climate variability, unsustainable ecosystem management (deforestation, poor farming), water overexploitation, and land degradation (erosion, mangrove loss). Producers lack capacity and climate info, hindering adaptation, leading to crop losses, food insecurity, and socio-economic instability.

Risks include implementation delays and associated cost overruns resulting from factors such as currency fluctuations, wage and material price increases, booming construction markets, and procurement/technical challenges. Additional risks are the potential failure of Cabinet to endorse sewerage regulations and cost-recovery mechanisms, which are conditions for supplementary financing effectiveness. Community opposition to land acquisition was a risk, though it is now complete.

Risks: Insufficient compilation of legal frameworks; lack of commitment/information from key public/private institutions; rotation of officials; disinterest from productive/academic sectors in training; limited access to information; changes in authorities hindering cooperation; public-private collaboration obstacles; limited institutional collaboration for capacity building; weak commitment from key institutions; interinstitutional coordination difficulties; limited documentation sharing; difficulty reaching agreements; lack of interest from key financial institutions.

1. Families and community organizations do not actively participate in the COCODE and COMUDE. 2. Local and central government entities generate bureaucracy and processes that block the execution of public funds and resources. 3. COCODEs do not designate representatives or actively participate in the Micro-basin Councils. 4. Local and central government entities do not participate in or attend to the requests of the local population expressed in the micro-basin management plan.

Relevant risks to the Cyclone Ian Recovery Project not achieving overall success include: more frequent or intense tropical cyclones or other natural hazards in the future; poor coordination between executing and implementing agencies and the agencies with oversight roles (Ministry of Public Enterprises, Ministry of Education and Training, and Tonga Energy Road Map Implementing Unit); and stretched administrative capacity and resources of the government, adversely affecting the implementation and effectiveness of the emergency assistance.

Relevant risks include: increases in equipment and material prices; extreme weather conditions and natural hazards damaging assets; non-full implementation of smart metering under the RDSS program; fragile financial position of UPCL, including inadequate tariff revisions and financial management weaknesses such as qualified audits, incomplete asset management, and lack of internal audit independence; implementing agencies' lack of experience with ADB-funded projects; congestion and capacity constraints in power evacuation and distribution networks; high distribution transformer failure rates and technical losses; obsolescence hindering renewable energy integration; and physical space constraints for network development in Dehradun.

Critical risks include: institutional coordination issues; financial risks in SWM due to difficult cost recovery and resort resistance; lack of community participation; MEEW's limited capacity in implementation, financial management, and Bank procurement, along with understaffing and weak environmental monitoring; failure to meet disbursement conditions (ESIA and operational framework for RWMF/IWMCs); challenges in siting RWMF due to land scarcity, NIMBY-type opposition, and potential adverse environmental impacts during construction/operation.

High climate vulnerability (droughts, floods) causes agricultural decline, food insecurity, and economic losses. Macroeconomic instability stems from persistent double-digit inflation, fiscal indiscipline, weak public financial management, external shocks, and volatile aid flows. Agricultural markets suffer from distortions, inefficient Farm Input Subsidy Program (FISP), ADMARC interventions, and poor rural integration. Governance issues include outdated land laws, weak fiduciary controls, limited institutional capacity, and risk of reform reversals. Humanitarian crises exacerbate fiscal pressure.

Political and Governance (High): Instability, armed groups, prior infrastructure damage. Macroeconomic (High): Low socio-economic development, high poverty, precarious utility finances, high operating costs, capital cost overruns. Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability (High): Weak policy, inadequate infrastructure, high technical/commercial losses (water 72% NRW, electricity 41.2% distribution), low collection rates (SODECA 50% cost recovery, ENERCA 55%), oversized staff, significant unpaid bills, poor operational/commercial management, lack of qualified staff. Fiduciary (Substantial): Low bill collection, large arrears, unreliable FM. Sector Strategies and Policies (Substantial): Weak policy, non-cost-reflective tariffs.

Substantial risks exist in political and governance, sector strategies/policies, technical design, institutional capacity for implementation and sustainability (including municipal financial unsustainability, weak O&M, and lack of skilled personnel), and fiduciary aspects. Procurement faces substantial risks including political interference, lack of bidders, and quality validation at decentralized levels. Other risks are rated high. Project implementation may suffer delays due to onerous preparation/approval processes and household reticence to connect. Environmental risks include water contamination, untreated sewage discharge, and construction impacts. Social risks involve public inconvenience, potential temporary displacement, and land acquisition for FSTPs.
